title: QP Oversight of Contract Manufacturing
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: Practical guidance on QP oversight CMO arrangements under EU GMP Chapter 7 and Annex 16: technical agreements, risk-based auditing and defensible batch certification.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner shot in a softly lit cleanroom corridor, cool teal and cyan tones blending into deep navy shadows, with a single subtle green accent glowing from a status indicator on stainless-steel manufacturing equipment. In the shallow-depth-of-field foreground, a gloved hand rests lightly on a sealed glass viewing panel into an aseptic filling line, blurred vials and a monitoring screen out of focus behind it. Soft natural daylight rakes across brushed metal and polished surfaces, conveying precision, oversight and quiet authority. 4:3 aspect ratio, premium editorial feel, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: Who is legally responsible for releasing a batch made by a CMO?
faqs.a: The certifying Qualified Person who signs the batch certification remains personally and legally responsible under Annex 16, regardless of where manufacture took place. A site QP at the CMO may release the batch for compliance with the dossier, but final certification for the EU or GB market rests with the certifying QP. That responsibility cannot be contracted away to the CMO.

faqs.q: How often should a CMO be audited?
faqs.a: Audit frequency should be risk-based in line with ICH Q9 rather than fixed by default. Stable, low-risk operations are commonly audited every two to three years, while sterile manufacture under Annex 1, complex dossiers or prior significant findings justify more frequent on-site audits. Crucially, no CMO should manufacture commercial product before an initial qualifying GMP audit.

faqs.q: What must a Chapter 7 technical agreement cover for QP oversight?
faqs.a: It must remove ambiguity over who decides, not merely list responsibilities. As a minimum it should define deviation and OOS handling with notification timelines, change-control approval boundaries, batch disposition versus certification roles, a prohibition on unapproved subcontracting, and guaranteed timely access to raw data. It should be reviewed whenever the process changes materially, not signed once and filed.
